item.title: Langflow CVE-2026-33017, Malicious AI Extensions, and GuardFall Agent Risks
item.content:encoded:
Langflow RCE Exploited to Deploy Monero Miner on Exposed AI App Endpoints — Must Read

Attackers are actively exploiting a critical unauthenticated Langflow RCE flaw to compromise exposed AI application endpoints and deploy a Monero miner. The malware also disables security controls, removes rival miners, establishes persistence, and can spread to other systems via reused SSH keys.

  • Threat actors are exploiting CVE-2026-33017, a critical unauthenticated remote code execution vulnerability in Langflow, to target exposed AI application endpoints and deploy a Monero cryptocurrency miner.
  • Trend Micro observed the campaign over a 19-day period between March 27 and April 15, 2026.
  • The attack uses attacker-supplied Python code in an unauthenticated Langflow API endpoint to download a shell script, fetch a miner binary called lambsys, and launch it as a detached process.
  • The lambsys malware terminates competing cryptocurrency miners, disables host-level security controls, establishes cron-based persistence, removes system logs, and can spread to SSH-reachable hosts using reused SSH keys.
  • The final payload contacts an external server to download a TAR archive containing a bespoke XMRig miner and queries ipinfo.io to obtain the victim host’s public IP address and location.

Silent Swap Crypto Clipper Uses Fake Google Notes Extension to Replace Wallet Addresses — Should Read

A malware campaign called Silent Swap is using fake browser extensions, including a bogus Google Notes utility, to secretly replace copied cryptocurrency wallet addresses with attacker-controlled ones. The campaign is notable for using blockchain-based C2 lookup and stealthy Chromium extension installation, making takedown and detection harder.

  • McAfee Labs identified an active cryptocurrency clipper campaign called Silent Swap that uses a malicious Chromium browser extension disguised as a benign “Google Notes” utility.
  • The Silent Swap installer has been observed in .NET and Golang variants and injects the malicious extension into detected Chromium-based browser profiles by modifying Secure Preferences and Preferences files.
  • The malicious extension can intercept wallet addresses copied to the clipboard and replace them with attacker-controlled addresses for Bitcoin, Ethereum, Bitcoin Cash, Ripple, Dash, and Solana transactions.
  • Silent Swap uses EtherHiding to retrieve active command-and-control server details from a blockchain smart contract, allowing operators to rotate infrastructure without redeploying the malware.
  • Socket reported malicious Chrome and Firefox extensions named “VPN Go: Free VPN” that provide visible proxy functionality while also monitoring and exfiltrating clipboard contents to attacker-controlled infrastructure.

GuardFall Exposes Open-Source AI Coding Agents to Decades-Old Shell Injection Risks — Should Read

Adversa AI’s GuardFall research shows that most tested open-source AI coding agents can be tricked into running dangerous shell commands because their safety filters check raw text instead of what bash actually executes. Users should avoid auto-execution and isolate agents from real home directories and secrets until stronger command parsing protections are added.

  • Adversa AI’s GuardFall research found that a decades-old shell trick bypassed safety checks in ten of eleven tested open-source coding and computer-use agents, with only Continue built to defend against it.
  • The bypass works because many agents check commands as plain text against blocklists, while bash later rewrites the text by stripping quotes and expanding shortcuts before execution.
  • An affected agent could run destructive or secret-stealing commands with the user’s full account access if it is pointed at a booby-trapped repository or software package.
  • Adversa reported that opencode, Goose, Cline, Roo-Code, Aider, Plandex, Open Interpreter, OpenHands, SWE-agent, and Hermes left the gap open, while Continue’s default editor mode blocked the tested payloads.
  • The article recommends reducing exposure by using a throwaway home folder, disabling auto-execute modes unless necessary, avoiding agents on pull requests from forks, and treating repository configuration files as untrusted code.

Fake Perplexity extension on Chrome Web Store tracked searches — Should Read

A fake Chrome extension impersonating Perplexity AI redirected users’ search queries through attacker-controlled infrastructure and collected browsing/search data. Microsoft says users who installed extension ID flkebkiofojicogddingbdmcmkpbplcd should remove it and consider rotating critical passwords.

  • A malicious Chrome Web Store extension called “Search for perplexity ai” masqueraded as the Perplexity AI answer engine.
  • Microsoft Threat Intelligence said the extension routed search queries and real-time suggestions through its own infrastructure before redirecting users to legitimate search services.
  • The extension changed browser search settings to replace the default search provider and intercept address-bar queries in Chromium browsers.
  • Microsoft found no evidence that the extension stole credentials or other sensitive information, but said its permissions would allow the operator to expand the scope of data theft.
  • Users who installed the extension with the ID “flkebkiofojicogddingbdmcmkpbplcd” should remove it and rotate critical account passwords out of caution.

House passes kids’ online safety bill, but Senate approval unlikely — May Read

The House passed the KIDS Act to strengthen children’s online safety rules, including age verification and data broker requirements, but the bill is unlikely to advance in the Senate. Key senators and advocates oppose it for lacking a stronger “duty of care” standard and raising privacy concerns.

  • The House passed the Kids Internet and Digital Safety Act with bipartisan support in a 267-117 vote, meeting the two-thirds majority required under the expedited voting process.
  • The bill faces a steep climb in the Senate, where many lawmakers support the competing Kids Online Safety Act, which includes a duty of care provision requiring big tech to prioritize children’s safety in platform design.
  • Sens. Richard Blumenthal and Marsha Blackburn criticized the House bill as a weak substitute for stronger accountability and pledged to block it in the Senate.
  • The KIDS Act would require AI chatbots to disclose they are not human, mandate age verification for users seeking to view porn, and impose requirements on data brokers handling children’s information.
  • Digital freedoms and civil liberties advocates condemned the bill’s age verification element as a threat to the privacy of all internet users, while ParentsSOS praised some provisions but said it would keep pushing for a duty of care.

Aikido acquires Root to secure the supply chain — May Read

Aikido has acquired Root and is launching Aikido Libraries and Aikido Images, which provide patched drop-in replacements for vulnerable open source libraries and container images without requiring disruptive upgrades or migrations. The company says critical fixes will also be contributed back upstream to strengthen the broader open source ecosystem.

  • Aikido has acquired Root, a company focused on generating precise CVE patches for the package versions teams actually run.
  • The article says open source software is a primary entry point for attackers, and nearly a third of known vulnerabilities are exploited on or before the day they are disclosed.
  • Root uses an agent-native, factory-like system to produce hundreds of verified CVE patches every day without introducing breaking changes, according to the article.
  • Aikido is launching Aikido Libraries and Aikido Images as vulnerability-free, drop-in replacement libraries and container images for software teams already use.
  • Aikido says critical fixes for actively exploited vulnerabilities will continue to be contributed back to the open source community rather than being locked behind a paywall.

Lessons from the Underground: How to Combat Business Email Compromise — Optional

Business Email Compromise has evolved into an organized, multi-step operation involving compromised SaaS/mail accounts, AI-generated communications, call-center pressure, and money-mule cash-out networks. Defenders should focus on protecting finance and leadership accounts, monitoring exposed credentials, and strengthening payment verification before fraudulent invoices arrive.

  • Business Email Compromise often begins with access to an organizational mailbox or business SaaS account, after which threat actors study organizational structure, financial privileges, procurement processes, internal conversations, vendor communications, and invoices.
  • Flare researchers found that underground actors are increasingly using AI to generate realistic business correspondence, mimic writing styles, and create context-aware payment requests or invoice fraud emails.
  • Threat actors highly value finance department email accounts because they can reveal accounts receivable, accounts payable, payrolls, invoices, overdue payments, and customer payment relationships.
  • Underground discussions described using call centers to pressure targeted businesses into finalizing fraudulent payments after fake invoices are sent.
  • The article states that cash-out is a major bottleneck for BEC because attackers need reliable business bank accounts, mule networks, or cash-out partners to move stolen money.

What the Black Hat NOC Taught Me About MCP & Agentic SOCs (Chapter 1 of 4) | Corelight — Optional

Corelight argues that lessons from the Black Hat network operations center show agentic SOCs and MCP-based integrations can help analysts work faster, but only if they are grounded in high-quality, trustworthy security telemetry.

  • The article is titled “What the Black Hat NOC Taught Me About MCP & Agentic SOCs.”
  • The article is published by Corelight.
  • The article is presented as Chapter 1 of a four-part series.
  • The article connects lessons from the Black Hat network operations center with MCP and agentic SOCs.

Distology becomes Value-Added Distributor of Radiflow in the Benelux — Optional

Distology is adding Radiflow’s OT cybersecurity platform to its Benelux portfolio as a value-added distributor. The partnership will give resellers and system integrators local access to Radiflow’s industrial threat detection, risk management and compliance tools for critical infrastructure sectors.

  • Distology has added Radiflow to its Benelux portfolio as a Value-Added Distributor.
  • Radiflow provides OT cybersecurity solutions for industrial sectors including energy, utilities, manufacturing, transport and chemicals.
  • Radiflow says its platform offers real-time threat detection based on risk analysis, automated risk management and support for compliance requirements.
  • Distology will make Radiflow’s solutions available to resellers and system integrators in the Benelux and support partners with knowledge, training and implementation tools.
  • Radiflow is targeting medium-sized organizations in critical infrastructure and industry that need OT cybersecurity but lack the resources of large enterprises.

item.title: SimpleHelp CVE-2026-48558 Exploitation, ToddyCat Umbrij, and Aflac Japan Breach Lead Digest
CVE-2026-33825 ransomware exploitation, Kemp LoadMaster RCE, and Glitch SPY Android RAT
